L1.area: User account management and HTTP request handling

L2.description: An integer overflow vulnerability exists where user-supplied passwords of arbitrary length are processed without a maximum length constraint. Submitting a password thousands of characters long triggers memory corruption and integer overflow during hashing, resulting in a denial of service.

L3.area: User registration, authentication, and password reset request validation
L3.description: An integer overflow vulnerability exists where user-supplied passwords of arbitrary length are accepted during registration, account creation, login, password update, and password reset operations without any maximum length validation. When an excessively long password (thousands of characters) is submitted to any of these endpoints, the password hashing operation triggers memory corruption and integer overflow, resulting in a denial of service.

ground_truth.reason: Password input fields across multiple user-facing forms lack maximum length validation, allowing submission of passwords thousands of characters long which causes integer overflow and memory corruption during password hashing.
